threats.title: Bot Admin Cannot Liquidate Unhealthy Yield Positions Due to Wrong Yield Account Resolution
threats.hypothesis_description: In _unstake and _repay, the yield account is resolved via yieldAccounts[msg.sender]. When botAdmin calls these functions to force-liquidate an unhealthy position, it resolves to yieldAccounts[botAdmin] which is likely address(0) or a completely different account, not the NFT owner's yield account (sd.yieldAccount). This causes reverts due to underflow when deducting yield shares from the wrong account, and protocol interactions (withdrawal requests, claims) operate against the wrong yield account in external protocols (Lido, EtherFi, sDAI).
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a code defect that renders the entire bot-driven liquidation mechanism non-functional. Unhealthy yield positions cannot be force-liquidated, leading to unbounded bad debt accumulation in the lending pool. The lending pool's depositors bear the loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) resolves to bot's account, not sd.yieldAccount; accountYieldShares[address(vars.yieldAccout)] -= sd.yieldShare underflows

preconditions: A user has staked via any yield strategy (Lido, EtherFi, sDAI)
The position health factor drops below nc.unstakeHeathFactor
botAdmin attempts to call unstake() or repay() to liquidate

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: vars.yieldAccout = IYieldAccount(yieldAccounts[msg.sender]) where msg.sender is botAdmin, not the NFT owner
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker stakes NFT, borrows maximum leverage, lets position go underwater; bot cannot liquidate
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker keeps borrowed funds while position becomes insolvent; lending pool absorbs the bad debt

economic_questions: What is the maximum bad debt per NFT position (leverageFactor * NFT value)?
How quickly can bad debt accumulate across multiple positions?
Can this drain a significant portion of the lending pool?
validation_requirements: Confirm yieldAccounts[botAdmin] == address(0) in production
Verify that botAdmin unstake/repay reverts in a test scenario
Calculate maximum extractable value per position given leverageFactor=50000 (500%)

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: 1 NFT (e.g., BAYC floor price ~10 ETH) + gas costs
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to 5x NFT value (50 ETH for a 10 ETH NFT with 500% leverage)
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single stake transaction, then wait for price movement

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient lending pool liquidity to borrow leveraged amount
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Before or during NFT price decline
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Each position creates bad debt equal to (borrowed amount - recoverable yield)

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All lending pool depositors for the affected asset (WETH/DAI)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their share of total supply
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Up to leverageFactor * total_NFT_value staked across all positions

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify that yieldAccounts[botAdmin] != sd.yieldAccount for all positions

threats.title: Yield Borrow Cap Inflated by Wrong Index (borrowIndex Instead of supplyIndex)
threats.hypothesis_description: In YieldLogic.executeYieldBorrowERC20, the total supply used for yield cap validation is computed as totalScaledCrossSupply.rayMul(groupData.borrowIndex), where groupData is the yield group. It should use assetData.supplyIndex instead. Since borrowIndex grows via compounded interest while supplyIndex grows via linear interest reduced by the fee factor, the borrowIndex is always >= supplyIndex. This inflates the computed totalSupply, making the yield cap check (totalBorrow <= totalSupply * yieldCap%) more permissive than intended.
threats.investigation_rationale: This code bug directly allows yield managers to borrow more than the configured percentage of actual deposited supply, potentially draining available liquidity beyond safe levels and creating systemic risk for regular depositors.
preconditions: Yield is enabled for pool and asset
Whitelisted yield manager has yieldCap set
Time has passed since pool creation so borrowIndex > supplyIndex
feeFactor > 0 (deployed with feeFactor=2000 i.e. 20%)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Yield manager borrows more than yieldCap% of actual deposits; the longer the pool runs, the wider the gap
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: With 20% yieldCap and borrowIndex 10% higher than supplyIndex, effective cap becomes ~22%, allowing ~10% extra borrowing

economic_questions: How fast does borrowIndex diverge from supplyIndex given deployed rate models?
What is the maximum excess borrow after 1 year of operation?
Could this cause liquidity shortfall for regular withdrawals?
validation_requirements: Simulate index divergence over time with deployed interest rate parameters
Calculate exact overshoot in yield borrowing relative to actual supply
Assess whether the excess borrow could drain available liquidity

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: NFTs for staking (covered by existing positions)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Additional yield earned on excess borrowed capital

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Available liquidity in pool must cover the excess borrow
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After extended period of high utilization (maximizes index divergence)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could prevent regular depositors from withdrawing if liquidity is over-extracted

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Pool depositors unable to withdraw due to drained liquidity
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional to their deposit share; withdrawal delays or failures
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Excess yield borrowing amount over intended cap

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: feeFactor=2000 (20%), yieldCap=2000 (20%), rate models deployed with base rates 1-10%
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compute borrowIndex vs supplyIndex divergence rate analytically

threats.title: Bot-Initiated Repay Blocked by Missing NFT Owner WETH Approval
threats.hypothesis_description: In _repay, when the claimed yield from the external protocol is less than the debt plus unstake fine, the contract attempts to pull the deficit from the NFT owner via underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount). When botAdmin initiates the repay for liquidation, the NFT owner may not have approved the YieldStaking contract, causing the transaction to revert. The NFT owner can deliberately revoke or never grant approval to block bot-driven liquidation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Even if ECON-H001 is fixed, this secondary issue can still block bot liquidation whenever claimed yield < debt + fine. A malicious user can ensure their position is always unliquidatable by simply not approving the YieldStaking contract for WETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: underlyingAsset.safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, address(this), vars.extraAmount) requires nftOwner approval which may not exist when botAdmin calls

preconditions: ECON-H001 yield account resolution bug is somehow fixed or worked around
Position is unhealthy and botAdmin initiates unstake+repay
Claimed yield from external protocol < total debt + unstake fine
NFT owner has not approved YieldStaking contract for WETH

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeTransferFrom(vars.nftOwner, ...) called without ensuring nftOwner has pre-approved the contract
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: User stakes NFT with maximum leverage, revokes any WETH approval, waits for position to go underwater
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User keeps borrowed funds; protocol cannot recover because repay reverts on transfer

economic_questions: Under what market conditions does claimed yield < debt + fine?
How often would this DoS vector actually be triggered?
What is the total exposure per blocked position?
validation_requirements: Test _repay with bot caller and no user approval when extraAmount > 0
Calculate typical scenarios where yield < debt at repay time
Assess if protocol has any fallback mechanism

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to leverageFactor * NFT value in unrecoverable debt
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high (borrowed funds retained, no repayment possible)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Pool must have liquidity for initial borrow
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During yield < borrow rate periods (negative carry)
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bad debt per position up to leveraged borrow amount

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all leveraged borrows from blocked positions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Yield vs borrow rate differential, maxUnstakeFine=100
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Simulate repay with extraAmount > 0 and no approval

threats.title: Unstake Fines Permanently Locked in YieldStaking Contract
threats.hypothesis_description: When botAdmin triggers an unstake with a fine, totalUnstakeFine is incremented and sd.unstakeFine is set. During _repay, the fine is deducted from claimed yield or pulled from the NFT owner, but it remains as WETH in the YieldStaking contract. The totalUnstakeFine counter is never decremented, and there is no withdrawal function for accumulated fines (no sweep for WETH, only emergencyEtherTransfer for native ETH in Lido/EtherFi). The fines are permanently locked.
threats.investigation_rationale: Protocol revenue from unstake fines is permanently lost. The WETH accumulates in the contract with no way to retrieve it. For YieldSavingsDai, DAI fines would be locked. This is a direct loss of protocol revenue.

proof_of_signal.pattern: totalUnstakeFine += unstakeFine in _unstake; never decremented in _repay; no sweep function for WETH/DAI

preconditions: botAdmin triggers unstake with unstakeFine > 0
Repay completes successfully collecting the fine

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: totalUnstakeFine only increases, never decreases; fine WETH/DAI sits in contract with no withdrawal path
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Not an attack per se, but a permanent loss of protocol revenue
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: All collected fines are locked forever; loss = sum of all fines collected

economic_questions: What is the expected fine collection rate over time?
Is there any indirect way to retrieve the locked WETH/DAI?
Can admin use rescue() on the YieldAccount to work around this?
validation_requirements: Verify no sweep/withdrawal function exists for WETH in YieldStaking contracts
Confirm emergencyEtherTransfer only handles native ETH, not WETH
Estimate total locked value based on expected fine frequency

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Cumulative fines: maxUnstakeFine (100 units) per liquidation event

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace WETH flow in _repay to confirm fine stays in contract

threats.title: Chainlink Oracle Staleness Allows Borrowing Against Overvalued Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: PriceOracle.getAssetPriceFromChainlink checks answer > 0, updatedAt != 0, and answeredInRound >= roundId, but does NOT enforce a maximum age check (block.timestamp - updatedAt < MAX_STALENESS). If a Chainlink aggregator stops updating (heartbeat failure), the protocol continues using the last reported price indefinitely. An attacker can exploit the gap between stale oracle price and actual market price to borrow against overvalued collateral or avoid liquidation.
threats.investigation_rationale: Without a staleness threshold, the protocol is vulnerable during any Chainlink feed outage. For volatile assets, even a few hours of stale pricing can create significant arbitrage opportunities. The protocol targets Ethereum mainnet and L2s where Chainlink feeds occasionally experience delays.
threats.config_evidence: PriceOracle.sol lines 81-89 show no time-based check; deployment script InitConfigPool.s.sol sets Chainlink aggregators for WETH, USDT, DAI

proof_of_signal.pattern: No check for (block.timestamp - updatedAt < MAX_STALENESS); only checks updatedAt != 0 and answeredInRound >= roundId

preconditions: Chainlink feed for an asset experiences update delay or heartbeat failure
Actual market price has diverged significantly from the stale oracle price
Sufficient pool liquidity to borrow against

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Only checks updatedAt != 0 which is always true for any previously-updated feed
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: During oracle outage: deposit collateral valued at stale high price, borrow maximum, profit when oracle updates

economic_questions: What is the historical frequency and duration of Chainlink feed delays?
What collateralFactor values are deployed (8050 for WETH)?
How much price divergence is needed for profitable exploitation?
validation_requirements: Check historical Chainlink heartbeat data for target assets
Calculate minimum profitable exploitation given gas costs and collateral factors
Assess whether BendNFTOracle has similar staleness issues

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Collateral equal to desired borrow amount * 10000/collateralFactor
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to price divergence during staleness window
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Depends on staleness duration and price volatility
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Must execute during the staleness window

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Sufficient pool liquidity for the borrow
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During market crash with oracle feed delay
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bad debt created equals stale-vs-actual price gap * borrowed amount

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All pool depositors for the affected asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Proportional share of bad debt created
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Up to available liquidity * price divergence ratio

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model exploitation profit for 1h, 4h, 24h staleness at various volatilities

threats.title: Dust Debt Rounding to Zero Allows Invisible Debt Accumulation
threats.hypothesis_description: In GenericLogic._getUserERC20DebtInBaseCurrency, the debt in base currency is calculated as (assetPrice * userTotalDebt) / (10 ** underlyingDecimals). For 18-decimal tokens with relatively low base currency prices, extremely small debts can truncate to 0 in base currency terms. An attacker could open many tiny borrow positions across different groups that individually round to 0 in the health factor calculation, accumulating real debt that is invisible to the solvency checks.
threats.investigation_rationale: If debt rounds to zero in the health factor calculation, the user appears to have infinite health factor despite having actual debt. This could allow bypassing collateral requirements through many dust-sized borrows across different groups.

proof_of_signal.pattern: userTotalDebt = assetPrice * userTotalDebt; return userTotalDebt / (10 ** assetData.underlyingDecimals) — integer division truncates small debts to 0

preconditions: Asset with 18 decimals and relatively low base currency price
Attacker can create many small borrow positions across groups
Each individual borrow is small enough that (price * scaledDebt * index) / 1e18 < 1

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: For 18-decimal token with price=1e8 (base currency unit), debt < 1e10 wei (~0.00000001 tokens) rounds to 0
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Open hundreds of dust-size borrows, each individually rounds to 0 debt in health factor
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Accumulate ~dust * N borrow positions that sum to non-trivial debt while health factor shows infinity

economic_questions: What is the minimum borrow amount enforced by the protocol?
How many dust borrows are needed to accumulate meaningful debt?
Is the gas cost of many small borrows prohibitive?
validation_requirements: Calculate exact dust threshold for each deployed asset given its price and decimals
Estimate gas costs for accumulating meaningful invisible debt
Check if borrow validation has minimum amount enforcement

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal collateral for dust borrows (if any collateral check passes with 0 debt)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: High gas cost for hundreds of transactions
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Limited by gas costs vs accumulated invisible debt value
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Potentially net negative after gas on L1, possibly viable on L2
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Multiple blocks for many borrow transactions

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Bounded by sum of all dust borrows

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Lending pool depositors if accumulated dust debt becomes bad debt
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Likely small per attacker; could be larger with coordination
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Bounded by available liquidity and gas economics

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: WETH price ~2000 USD (1e8 units), 18 decimals; dust threshold = 1e10 wei ~= 0.00000001 WETH ~= 0.00002 USD
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify with concrete asset prices whether meaningful debt accumulation is gas-efficient

threats.title: SavingsDai Yield Underflow Blocks All New Stakes When External Protocol Value Drops
threats.hypothesis_description: YieldSavingsDai.getAccountTotalUnstakedYield computes balance - accountYieldInWithdraws[account], where balance = sdai.balanceOf(account). If the sDAI balance drops below the tracked in-withdrawal amount (e.g., due to MakerDAO DSR reduction, governance action, or sDAI exploit), this subtraction underflows in Solidity 0.8+, causing a revert. This blocks ALL new stake operations for any NFT using the affected yield account, and also blocks yield share calculations needed by other functions.
threats.investigation_rationale: The sDAI yield account balance could decrease due to external protocol actions outside of BendDAO's control. This creates a DoS condition where users cannot stake new positions and potentially cannot interact with existing positions that require yield share calculations.
deployment_assumptions: sDAI yield protocol experiences value decrease such that account balance < tracked withdrawals
threats.config_evidence: YieldSavingsDai.sol deployed at 0x5F695a92C0B3A595ceE43750C433e7B1109CBe3C per deployment scripts

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(balance >= inWithdraw) reverts if sDAI balance drops below tracked in-withdrawal amounts; balance - inWithdraw underflow

preconditions: User has an active unstake with withdrawAmount tracked in accountYieldInWithdraws
sDAI balance of the yield account decreases (DSR change, protocol issue)
balance < accountYieldInWithdraws[account]

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: require(balance >= inWithdraw) with external dependency on sDAI.balanceOf() which is not under protocol control
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: External protocol change (not attacker-initiated) causes DoS
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; DoS prevents new stakes and may block health factor calculations

economic_questions: Has MakerDAO ever reduced DSR mid-period causing sDAI value decrease?
What is the maximum possible sDAI balance decrease in a single governance action?
Can the DoS cascade to affect other protocol functions?
validation_requirements: Test getAccountTotalUnstakedYield when balance < inWithdraw
Check if health factor calculations for existing positions also call this function
Assess recovery path if DoS occurs

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until external protocol condition resolves or admin intervention

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During MakerDAO governance changes to DSR
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All sDAI yield staking operations blocked for affected accounts

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users with active sDAI yield positions in unstake state
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Cannot create new positions or interact with yield share accounting
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Existing positions may be stuck in limbo until resolved

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: sDAI share price trajectory, MakerDAO DSR change history

threats.title: External Callers Can Directly Borrow From Yield Pool Without YieldStaking Collateral
threats.hypothesis_description: The Yield.yieldBorrowERC20 function is publicly callable by any address through the Yield module proxy. The only authorization is that the caller's address must have yieldCap > 0 in the asset's yieldManagerLookup. If the pool admin has set yieldCap for an address (e.g., a YieldStaking contract), AND that address has its own externally accessible entry points, OR if yieldCap is misconfigured for a non-manager address, that address can borrow directly from the yield pool without posting any collateral via the YieldStaking flow.
threats.investigation_rationale: The yield borrow mechanism relies on the YieldStaking contract as the enforcement layer for collateral. If someone can call yieldBorrowERC20 directly (bypassing YieldStaking), they get uncollateralized loans. The pool admin must carefully manage yieldCap assignments.
deployment_assumptions: yieldCap is set for an address that can also directly call yieldBorrowERC20 without going through proper collateral flow
threats.config_evidence: InitConfigYield.s.sol sets yieldCap for YieldStaking addresses via configurator.setManagerYieldCap; Yield.sol yieldBorrowERC20 has no additional collateral check beyond cap

proof_of_signal.pattern: No collateral verification in yield borrow - only checks yield caps, not whether caller has posted NFT collateral

preconditions: Caller has yieldCap > 0 set by admin for their address
Caller can call yieldBorrowERC20 through Yield proxy directly
Sufficient pool liquidity available

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: validateYieldBorrowERC20 only checks caps, not collateral backing
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: If yieldCap is set for any address that isn't a properly-secured yield manager, that address can drain liquidity
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Borrow up to yieldCap% of total supply without any collateral

economic_questions: Can the YieldStaking contract address itself be used to borrow directly via the proxy?
Are there any other addresses with yieldCap that could be exploited?
What prevents arbitrary callers from having yieldCap set?
validation_requirements: Verify that only admin can set yieldCap
Check if YieldStaking address calling through Yield proxy bypasses its own collateral checks
Test direct yieldBorrowERC20 call from a whitelisted address

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to yieldCap% of total supply
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Near infinite if successful (no capital required)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Direct loss of lending pool liquidity

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Up to yieldCap% of their deposits
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: yieldCap% of total pool supply (deployed at 20%)

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Attempt direct yieldBorrowERC20 from whitelisted address

threats.title: Interest Rate Index uint128 Overflow Permanently Bricks Asset Operations
threats.hypothesis_description: Supply and borrow indices are stored as uint128 and updated via SafeCast.toUint128(). Starting from 1e27 (RAY), the maximum growth factor before overflow is ~3.4e11x. While this seems large, a misconfigured interest rate model returning extremely high rates, or a prolonged period without any interaction on a high-rate asset, could cause the index to overflow. Once this happens, all operations on the asset (deposit, withdraw, borrow, repay) permanently revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: The overflow is irreversible - there is no admin recovery mechanism. The asset becomes permanently bricked, locking all user funds. Even legitimate rate models could produce dangerously high indices over long time horizons without user interaction to reset the timestamp.
deployment_assumptions: Interest rate model returns extremely high rates, or asset goes without interaction for extended period
threats.config_evidence: InterestLogic.sol uses .toUint128() for both supplyIndex and borrowIndex; deployed rate models have base rates up to 10% with slope up to 100%

proof_of_signal.pattern: assetData.supplyIndex = nextSupplyIndex.toUint128() and groupData.borrowIndex = nextBorrowIndex.toUint128() — SafeCast reverts on overflow with no recovery

preconditions: Interest rate model returns very high rates (>100% APY compounded)
No interaction with the asset for an extended period
borrowIndex * compoundedInterest exceeds type(uint128).max

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: toUint128() will revert when index exceeds ~3.4e38; starting from 1e27, this allows ~3.4e11x growth
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set up conditions where no one interacts with asset while high rates compound; or admin sets malicious rate model
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit; permanent DoS locks all user funds in the asset

economic_questions: At maximum deployed rate (100% slope), how long until overflow?
Can the rate model be manipulated to return rates that cause faster overflow?
What are the time bounds for different rate scenarios?
validation_requirements: Calculate maximum time-to-overflow for deployed rate model parameters
Test what happens when toUint128 reverts in updateInterestIndexs
Verify no recovery mechanism exists

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Depends on rate model; potentially years at normal rates, much less with extreme rates

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When asset has high utilization (high rates) and no activity
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All deposited and borrowed funds for the asset permanently frozen

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All depositors and borrowers of the affected asset
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% of their deposited/borrowed amount in that asset
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total supply + total borrow for the asset

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Max rate = baseRate(10%) + slope(100%) at 100% utilization = 110% APY compound
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: At 110% APY: 1.1^n * 1e27 > 3.4e38 → n > log(3.4e11)/log(1.1) ≈ 279 years (safe at normal rates)

threats.title: Fee Collection Mints Treasury Supply Before Index Update Creating Temporary Accounting Gap
threats.hypothesis_description: In PoolLogic.executeCollectFeeToTreasury, accruedFee is read and zeroed BEFORE InterestLogic.updateInterestIndexs is called. Then the normalized income (post-update) is used to convert the pre-update fee to actual tokens. While the conversion math is correct, the sequence means that if the supply cap check in validateDepositERC20 runs within the same block after fee collection, the increased totalScaledCrossSupply (from treasury minting) plus the accrued fee reset to 0 could create a window where the supply cap check uses stale accounting, potentially allowing deposits that slightly exceed the cap.
threats.investigation_rationale: The supply cap validation in validateDepositERC20 uses (totalScaledSupply + accruedFee).rayMul(supplyIndex). After fee collection, accruedFee is 0 but totalScaledCrossSupply has increased by the collected fee amount. This should be equivalent, but the timing of index update vs fee reset could create edge cases.

proof_of_signal.pattern: accruedFee read and zeroed before updateInterestIndexs; then normalizedIncome used to compute amountToCollect which is minted to treasury supply

preconditions: Fee collection is triggered by pool admin
Supply cap is close to being reached
A deposit transaction executes in the same block after fee collection

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: accruedFee set to 0 before index update; new fee from update stored separately; supply cap check in deposit may see different accounting
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin collects fee, then deposit in same block could bypass cap by small margin
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Marginal supply cap bypass; impact limited to the fee amount

economic_questions: Does the accounting perfectly balance after fee collection?
Is there a rounding difference between fee-as-accruedFee vs fee-as-treasurySupply?
Could the supply cap be exceeded by a meaningful amount?
validation_requirements: Compare totalScaledCrossSupply + accruedFee before vs after fee collection
Check if rounding in rayMul/rayDiv creates any discrepancy
Quantify maximum possible supply cap overshoot

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Marginal (up to fee amount worth of excess supply)

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Large deposit near supply cap threshold
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Same block as admin fee collection
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Minimal - supply cap exceeded by at most fee amount

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Formal verification of accounting equivalence before/after fee collection
